Week of 10/13 - 10/24

Weekly Market Summary

By Noah Halfon

Global Weekly Market Movements

U.S. Equities

  • S&P 500 Index: 6,791.69 (1.52%)
    • SPX reaches a new record high after fully recovering last week’s -3% pullback, with most sectors this week finishing green. Leading the gains were XLK technology (3.01%) and XLE energy (2.43%). Last week's pullback created a massive high beta drawdown, but has rebounded amid positive CPI and earnings. We look ahead to next week's rate cut decision and more big tech earnings as buyers keep stepping in on dips. Are momentum names starting to slow down?
  • NASDAQ Composite: 23,204.87 (1.58%)
  • Dow Jones Industrial Average: 47,207.12 (1.93%)
    • The blue chip DOW rallied over 400 points to close above 47,000 for the first time. Caused by softer CPI data boosting hopes for a fed rate cut, large cap tech and semiconductor stocks led this rally pushing the index to new highs.
  • Russell 3000 Index: 3,863.16 (1.67%)

Big Movers of the Week

    • Intuitive Surgical Inc (ISRG): 546.61 (20.78%)
      • ISRG saw shares jump 23% after beating earnings expectations. Revenue came in at $2.5 billion, up 23% YoY, placing over 400 surgical robots in hospitals. EBIT margins also rose to 29%, while the company also repurchased 4 million shares this quarter.
    • General Motors Company (GM): 69.66 (18.97%)
      • Strong earnings boosted shares of GM as they reported the highest Q3 market share since 2017. They also beat revenue expectations while updating their full year profit guidance, signalling confidence in their business despite tariff troubles and slowing overseas EV sales. Despite this, the average US transaction price on new vehicles has risen to $50,000 in September.
    • Molina Healthcare Inc (MOH): 163.32 (-14.94%)
      • Molina stock dropped 20% in hours after the healthcare company slashed its annual earnings guidance. The company missed badly on EPS, recording $1.84 vs the $3.89 analysts expected, while Q4 EPS expectations have also been cut by more than $2. Rising medical expenses and changing government pricing and regulatory changes continues to contribute to a broader struggle in the healthcare sector.

Chinese Equities - Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP): 3,950.31 (2.19%)

Hong Kong Equities - Hang Seng Index (HSI): 26,160.15 (1.07%)

Japanese Equities- Nikkei 225 (NI225): 49,299.65 (2.00%)

European Equities

  • UK Index (UKX): 9,645.62 (3.11%)
  • German Index (DAX): 24,239.89 (0.85%)

Commodities

  • Gold Futures: 4,126.90 (-3.52%)
  • Silver Futures: 48.41 (-4.99%)
    • After hitting consistent all time highs for the past month, gold and silver have seen their steepest selloff in years this past week. Spot gold declined 6.3%, the largest decline in 12 years, while silver lost 8.7%. Interestingly, global central banks have begun holding 20% of their assets in gold, but many may see digital gold as a better hedge against currency debasement.
  • Crude Oil Futures: 61.44 (7.85%)

Crypto

  • Bitcoin (BTC): 113,622.50 (4.84%)
  • Ethereum (ETH): 4,069.94 (5.04%)

U.S. Monetary & Fiscal Policy

FOMC: The Fed is once again projected to cut rates by another quarter point at next week's meeting. Also, after more than three years of shrinking its balance sheet, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell hinted that the central bank's long term effort of decreasing its balance sheet (also known as quantitative tightening) may be nearing its end. He remarked that QT could conclude soon to prevent strain in short-term funding markets, as well as reinforcing expectations for a cut by saying that labor market prospects continue to worsen. Inflation concerns also remain a major focus amid the ongoing government shutdown.

Trade:

US/China: US China trade tensions have escalated recently after China reportedly takes a hostile trade stance on rare earth minerals and refuses to buy US soybeans. The US retaliated by commenting on not buying Chinese cooking oil, while Secretary Scott Bessent voiced that the World Bank should end support for China over rare earth minerals. More recently, the Chinese Embassy has posted that agreements have been made and that the two sides have reached a new framework. Secretary Bessent also noted that the President’s firm stance on rare-earth export controls and his use of tariff threats helped avert escalation. Markets go green after easing geopolitical tensions and a confirmed October 30th in person meeting between the two leaders.

Trump/Canada: President Trump has terminated all trade talks with Canada after claiming Canada produced a fraudulent ad speaking out on tariffs. Now implementing an additional 10% tariff on Canadian goods, total US tariffs stand at 35%, with energy products at 10%. Markets remained generally unchanged after this exchange.

Russia/India: Crude oil saw prices jump after the US sanctioned Russian oil producers in hopes of pressuring Moscow over the conflict in Ukraine. The move is expected to tighten global supply, particularly affecting shipments to China and India, two of Russia’s largest oil buyers. India is also in talks to lower export tariffs with the US in exchange for reducing its purchases of Russian crude and allowing more American corn and soymeal imports. Energy markets responded quickly to this news, with oil futures climbing and energy stocks rallying. Will India be able to resist Russian oil prices?

US/Australia: Trump and Australian Prime Minister Albanese have agreed to secure a more steady supply of rare earth minerals and materials in hopes of reducing supply chain reliance on China. The U.S. Export Import Bank (EXIM) also approved letters of interest totaling $2.2 billion, unlocking billions in investment for projects that enhance supply chain security and support the mining and materials sectors. This could be a good opportunity to gain exposure to international names and upside following the recent sector pullback. Many rare earth stocks have pulled back these last two weeks as investors sell amid US-China tensions and take profit after short term price runups. Minerals still remain my top sector pick, with an increasing number of catalysts steadily building momentum, and US policy continuously aimed at undermining reliance on China.

Impacted Equities: ARAAF, VHMLF, UUUU, ILKAF, LYSDY, MP, NOURF, ARAFF

Global Macroeconomic News

Bank Earnings: Big banks reported strong quarterly results last week, with Goldman Sachs’ reporting record revenue. Goldman remarked that a fast pace of growth in its investment arm is outpacing rivals. Citi also beat estimates across all five of its business lines, while Wells Fargo raised its profitability metric, its first upgrade since removing regulatory restraints. Strong earnings for banks are a positive for the economy as it signals healthy spending and loan demand, while strong balance sheets support overall market sentiment.

Credit Scare: Stocks slid last week after news of regional banks reporting millions in fraud losses inciting credit fears and mass selling. Zion’s Bancorp reported a record $60 million credit provision and a charge off of $50 million, causing shares to fall -9.2%, while Western Alliance filed a lawsuit against a borrower claiming fraud saw losses of -8.7%. Fintech firms across the board also slipped, and major firm Jefferies dropped -7.9%. Credit concerns are always a reason to sell first and ask questions later, as borrowing money is essential for everyday purchases. Underlying credit structure concerns ask the question; are regional bank credit concerns harmful enough for the entire economy to be afraid? Are we seeing SVB again?

CPI: September CPI comes in at 3.0% vs 3.1% expected, the highest reading since January but still under expectations. The index for gasoline was up 4.1% for the month of September, with Energy pushing to 2.8%, while Food was up 3.1%. A positive for rate cuts as well, equities jumped after this news, with the S&P hitting new highs on Friday's close.

IMF: The International Monetary Fund has warned that the global economy is showing signs of strain and may be slowing down. Specifically citing US policy as a root cause, they remarked that tariffs are hurting global trade and relations. The global economy is projected to grow at 3.2% this year, actually up from 3.0% earlier in July, but is projected to drop to 3.1% in 2026 as tariff effects come through. Other concerns include worldwide high inflation readings and excessive government spending that is contributing to higher interest rates and worse outlooks.

Sources: Google Finance, Market Watch, CME Fedwatch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters, New York Times, Bloomberg, Wall St Journal, Washington Post, US Department of the Treasury, GuruFocus, Edward Jones

Noah Halfon
